_metadata.description: Ground truth pH and water activity values for Monte Carlo stochasticity test. 15 foods across 3 difficulty tiers. ALL values sourced from the project's corrected food_properties.csv, which was audited against original PDF sources.
_metadata.rebuild_reason: Previous version (v0.1) contained values from LLM training data with fabricated source attributions. This version uses ONLY values from the audited food_properties.csv.
_metadata.notes: Difficulty tiers reflect how consistently LLMs reproduce the correct pH from training data. Easy foods are heavily represented in food safety literature. Hard foods are fermented, less common, or have unusually wide pH ranges that LLMs struggle to pin down.

foods.name: chicken
foods.notes: Raw chicken. Well-documented in food safety literature. pH well above 4.6 boundary.

foods.name: whole cow milk
foods.aw_note: Typical for fresh dairy; no food-specific aw in source CSV
foods.notes: Common food, stable pH, well above 4.6. Baseline for low-variance reference.

foods.name: apple (eating)
foods.csv_entry: Range across apple varieties: Delicious 3.90, Golden Delicious 3.60, Jonathan 3.33, McIntosh 3.34, Winesap 3.47
foods.notes: Range spans multiple apple varieties in the FDA list. pH clearly below 4.6 TCS threshold.

foods.name: ground beef
foods.notes: Raw ground beef. Wide range in source (5.1-6.2) but well above 4.6.

foods.name: eggs (whole, fresh)
foods.csv_entry: eggs whole new laid,eggs,6.58,6.58,,,Whole fresh eggs,FDA-PH-2007
foods.notes: Single value in source (not a range). Well above 4.6.

foods.name: yogurt
foods.aw_note: Typical for fresh dairy; no food-specific aw in source CSV
foods.notes: Fermented dairy. Below 4.6 but close enough that LLM variance could approach boundary. LLMs may confuse plain vs Greek vs flavored yogurt pH.

foods.name: cottage cheese
foods.aw_note: Typical for fresh dairy; no food-specific aw in source CSV
foods.notes: Just ABOVE the 4.6 boundary. If an LLM returns pH 4.3, it incorrectly classifies this as an acid food.

foods.name: buttermilk
foods.aw_note: Typical for fresh dairy; no food-specific aw in source CSV
foods.notes: CRITICAL: Range straddles pH 4.6 boundary. Even the authoritative source shows this food spans acid/low-acid. LLM variance here is guaranteed to produce boundary crossings.

foods.name: fresh salmon
foods.notes: Fresh salmon (not smoked or canned). Wide range. LLMs may confuse fresh vs smoked vs canned salmon pH.

foods.name: tomatoes
foods.notes: THE classic boundary food. Range straddles 4.6. Subject of decades of food safety debate. LLMs will reflect the controversy with high variance.

foods.name: sauerkraut
foods.aw_note: Estimated for fermented vegetable; not in source CSV
foods.notes: Fermented cabbage. Narrow range in source but LLMs may confuse with fresh cabbage (pH 5.2-6.8) or with pre-fermentation pH.

foods.name: soy sauce
foods.aw_note: Estimated for fermented condiment; not in source CSV. High salt content lowers aw significantly.
foods.notes: Fermented condiment. Very wide range (1.0 pH unit). Straddles 4.6 boundary. LLMs may conflate different types (light vs dark vs tamari).

foods.name: olives (green, fermented)
foods.aw_note: Estimated for brined olives; not in source CSV
foods.notes: Fermented food with very wide range (1.0 pH unit). Upper bound is exactly at 4.6 boundary. LLMs may confuse with black olives (pH 6.0-7.0) or ripe olives (pH 6.0-7.5).

foods.name: persimmons
foods.notes: Uncommon fruit that straddles the 4.6 boundary. LLMs have limited training data on persimmon pH. High expected variance.

foods.name: mangoes (ripe)
foods.notes: Widest range of any fruit in the FDA list (1.4 pH units). Depends heavily on variety and ripeness. Range crosses 4.6 boundary. LLMs will give highly variable answers.

propagation_scenario.description: Fixed scenario for propagating pH variance to growth predictions. We hold everything constant except pH, then compute mu_max for each sampled pH value. This isolates the effect of pH uncertainty on the prediction.
propagation_scenario.notes: 25C for 4 hours is a common room-temperature abuse scenario. Salmonella growth rate is sensitive to pH in the 4.0-7.0 range. Holding aw constant at 0.99 isolates the pH effect.
